citation: Witzel, M. (1995). Rgvedic history: poets, chieftains and polities. In G. Erdosy (Ed.), The Indo-Aryans of Ancient South Asia. De Gruyter. Estimates 1500-1200 BCE for core Rigvedic hymns.

concepts_introduced.note: cosmic order, predecessor of dharma
sacred utterance/power, not yet the Upanishadic absolute
breath/self, not yet the philosophical absolute self
magical power of the gods, Indra's creative illusion — RV 6.47.18
sacrifice as cosmic principle
ritual drink, cosmic principle

citation: Griffith, R.T.H. (1895). Hymns of the Atharvaveda. Scholarly consensus places it later than Rigveda. Whitney, W.D. (1905). Atharva-Veda Samhita. Harvard Oriental Series.

concepts_introduced.note: beginning transition toward cosmic principle
breath as life principle

citation: Olivelle, P. (1998). The Early Upanisads. Oxford University Press. Places Brihadaranyaka and Chandogya as the earliest Upanishads, pre-Buddhist, around 800-600 BCE. Also Ranade, R.D. (1926). A Constructive Survey of Upanishadic Philosophy.

concepts_introduced.note: first systematic identification of brahman with ultimate reality — BAU 1.4.10
aham brahmasmi — I am brahman — BAU 1.4.10
not this, not this — negative theology — BAU 3.9.26
consciousness is brahman
cycle of rebirth — early formulation
early Upanishadic karma doctrine — BAU 3.2.13 — one of the earliest textual sources

citation: Olivelle, P. (1998). The Early Upanisads. Oxford University Press. Chandogya placed alongside Brihadaranyaka as earliest stratum.

concepts_introduced.note: that thou art — CU 6.8.7 — identity of atman and brahman
being as ultimate — CU 6.2.1

citation: Jacobi, H. (1884). Jaina Sutras Part I. Sacred Books of the East Vol. 22. Oxford. Scholarly consensus places Acaranga Sutra as the oldest surviving Jain canonical text, contemporary with early Buddhism.

concepts_introduced.note: non-violence as absolute principle — foundational Jain ethics
living soul — Jain technical term distinct from Vedic atman
non-living matter — Jain ontological category
Jain karma as literal subtle matter binding the soul — distinct from Vedic karma as ritual action
Jain moksha — soul's release from karmic matter
ascetic practice as means of liberation
liberation as soteriological goal — appears in Jain Acaranga Sutra and early Buddhist texts before Upanishadic formulation — see Bronkhorst (2007)

citation: Gombrich, R. (1988). Theravada Buddhism. Routledge. Dates Buddha to ca. 480-400 BCE. Wynne, A. (2007). The Origin of Buddhist Meditation. Routledge. Norman, K.R. (1983). Pali Literature. Wiesbaden: Harrassowitz.

concepts_introduced.note: no-self — SN 22.59 Anattalakkhana Sutta — no permanent self in any aggregate
impermanence — all conditioned phenomena are impermanent
suffering/unsatisfactoriness — first noble truth
Pali for nirvana — cessation of craving and suffering
dependent origination — SN 12 Nidana-samyutta — things arise in dependence
Buddha's teaching and cosmic law — distinct from Vedic dharma as ritual duty
five aggregates — form sensation perception formations consciousness — no self found in any
AN 6.63 — karma is cetana, intention — radical reinterpretation away from ritual action
monastic community as third jewel
ethical conduct as foundation of practice
meditative concentration — jhana stages in Pali canon
wisdom — third element of noble eightfold path

citation: Larson, G.J. (1979). Classical Samkhya. Delhi: Motilal Banarsidass. The Samkhyakarika is the earliest surviving systematic Samkhya text though the school is older. Burley, I. (2007). Classical Samkhya and Yoga. Routledge.

concepts_introduced.note: pure consciousness — Samkhya's term for the witnessing self — distinct from Vedantic atman
primal nature/matter — all that is not pure consciousness
another name for prakriti as primal unmanifest matter — this is Samkhya not Advaita
three qualities sattva rajas tamas constituting all matter
discriminative knowledge — distinguishing purusha from prakriti
cosmic intelligence — first evolute of prakriti

citation: Larson, G.J. & Bhattacharya, R.S. (1987). Samkhya: A Dualist Tradition in Indian Philosophy. Princeton. Philipp Maas (2013). A Concise Historiography of Classical Yoga Philosophy, dates Yogasutra to 4th-5th century CE.

concepts_introduced.note: cessation of mental fluctuations — YS 1.2 — definition of yoga
eight limbs — yama niyama asana pranayama pratyahara dharana dhyana samadhi
surrender to Ishvara — theistic element added to Samkhya dualism
aloneness of pure consciousness — Yoga's term for liberation distinct from Buddhist nirvana and Vedantic moksha

citation: Kalupahana, D.J. (1986). Nagarjuna: The Philosophy of the Middle Way. SUNY Press. Garfield, J.L. (1995). The Fundamental Wisdom of the Middle Way. Oxford. Scholarly consensus places Nagarjuna at ca. 150-250 CE.

concepts_introduced.note: MMK ch. 24 — all phenomena empty of svabhava (own-nature) — systematic Madhyamaka emptiness distinct from earlier Buddhist usage
refutation of inherent existence — MMK 15 — direct critique of Brahmanical substance ontology
MMK 24.8-10 — samvrti (conventional) and paramartha (ultimate) — systematic formulation
MMK 24.18 — dependent origination IS emptiness — crucial identification
MMK 18 — explicit philosophical refutation of Brahmanical atman
conceptual proliferation — what sunyata is free of

citation: Kochumuttom, T.A. (1982). A Buddhist Doctrine of Experience. Delhi: Motilal Banarsidass. Schmithausen, L. (1987). Alayavijnana. Tokyo: International Institute for Buddhist Studies.

concepts_introduced.note: storehouse consciousness — Yogacara's 8th consciousness — holds karmic seeds — no Vedantic parallel before this
representation-only — external world is mental representation — idealist Buddhist epistemology
three natures — imagined dependent perfected — Yogacara ontology
tathagatagarbha — all beings have buddha nature — Yogacara-related development

citation: King, R. (1995). Early Advaita Vedanta and Buddhism. SUNY Press. This is the key text demonstrating Buddhist influence on pre-Shankara Advaita. Karmarkar, R.D. (1953). Gaudapada-karika. Poona.

concepts_introduced.note: doctrine of no-origination — GK 3 — directly influenced by Madhyamaka — Gaudapada uses Buddhist arguments to defend Advaita
GK 1.17 — Gaudapada systematises maya drawing on Buddhist mayopama (illusion-like) — this is the key moment of Buddhist-to-Vedanta transfer your teacher identified
perception-creation — world exists only in perception — clearly Yogacara influenced
fourth state beyond waking dreaming deep sleep — systematic formulation

text: Brahmasutra Bhashya and Upanishad commentaries — Shankara
citation: Deutsch, E. (1969). Advaita Vedanta: A Philosophical Reconstruction. East-West Center Press. Mayeda, S. (1992). A Thousand Teachings: The Upadesasahasri of Sankara. SUNY Press. Dates 788-820 CE are traditional and accepted by most scholars.

concepts_introduced.note: Shankara systematises Gaudapada's maya into full cosmological doctrine — his Vedantin critics (Ramanuja etc) explicitly note the Buddhist origin of this move
theory of apparent transformation — world is apparent modification not real transformation of brahman
superimposition — false attribution of properties — BSBh intro
brahman without qualities as ultimate — building on Upanishads but systematised against Buddhist sunyata
individual soul is ultimately identical to brahman — mahavakya doctrine systematised
ignorance as root cause of samsara — Shankara's Advaita reinterpretation borrowing Buddhist avidya

citation: Carman, J.B. (1974). The Theology of Ramanuja. Yale University Press. Lester, R.C. (1976). Ramanuja on the Yoga. Madras: Adyar Library.

concepts_introduced.note: qualified non-dualism — brahman as qualified by souls and world as its body
body-soul relationship between world/souls and brahman
self-surrender to Ishvara as path — Sri Vaishnava theology
contra Shankara — brahman with qualities is truly ultimate not a lower level concession

citation: Sharma, B.N.K. (1962). Philosophy of Sri Madhvacarya. Bombay: Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan. Stoker, V. (2016). Polemics and Patronage in the City of Victory. University of California Press.

concepts_introduced.note: five eternal differences — God-soul, God-matter, soul-soul, soul-matter, matter-matter
independent vs dependent reality — only Vishnu is truly independent
gradation of souls — hierarchical ontology of liberated souls

citation: Dimock, E.C. & Stewart, T.K. (1999). Caitanya Caritamrta of Krsnadasa Kaviraja. Harvard Oriental Series. Haberman, D.L. (1988). Acting as a Way of Salvation. Oxford.

concepts_introduced.note: inconceivable simultaneous difference and non-difference — Chaitanya's synthesis
divine love as highest goal — surpassing moksha
Krishna's divine play as the nature of ultimate reality
aesthetic-devotional experience as path to the divine

text: Brahmasutra commentary and Yoga Sutras commentary — Various Buddhist parallels
citation: Bronkhorst, J. (2007). Greater Magadha. Brill. Key thesis: karma, rebirth, and ascetic liberation doctrines originated in the Shramana traditions (Jain and Buddhist) and were absorbed into Brahmanical thought. Larson, G.J. (1969). The Concept of the Body in Samkhya-Yoga. Philosophy East and West. Chapple, C. (1993). Nonviolence to Animals, Earth, and Self in Asian Traditions. SUNY Press.

concepts_introduced.note: The systematic karmic doctrine as ethical causation (not just ritual efficacy) first appears in Shramana traditions. Bronkhorst (2007) argues this was a Shramana (Jain/Buddhist) innovation absorbed into Brahmanical thought

The concept of liberation from the cycle of rebirth as a soteriological goal appears in Jain and early Buddhist texts before the Upanishadic formulations. See Olivelle (1996) Upanisads, Oxford, who notes the Upanishadic moksha doctrine develops in dialogue with Shramana traditions
While ahimsa appears in late Vedic texts, its elevation to a supreme principle is widely attributed to Jain and Buddhist influence on Brahmanical thought — Dundas (2002) The Jains, Routledge

text: Vimalakirti Nirdesa Sutra and Lankavatara Sutra
citation: Thurman, R.A.F. (1976). The Holy Teaching of Vimalakirti. Penn State Press. Suzuki, D.T. (1932). The Lankavatara Sutra. Routledge. Red Pine (2004). The Diamond Sutra. Counterpoint.

concepts_introduced.note: buddha nature — all beings contain the seed of buddhahood — Mahayana development
remaining in samsara to liberate all beings — Mahayana ethics
skillful means — teachings adapted to the capacity of the listener
emptiness is itself empty — prevents reification of the concept

text: Tibetan canon — Kangyur and Tengyur translations
citation: Kapstein, M.T. (2000). The Tibetan Assimilation of Buddhism. Oxford. Davidson, R.M. (2002). Indian Esoteric Buddhism. Columbia University Press.

concepts_introduced.note: Tibetan — natural state of awareness — Dzogchen tradition — distinct from Indian sunyata though related
great perfection — Nyingma tradition — primordial purity and spontaneous presence
transformation of afflictive emotions rather than renunciation — Vajrayana method
reincarnated lama — distinctly Tibetan Buddhist institution

text: Platform Sutra — Huineng; Blue Cliff Record
citation: Yampolsky, P.B. (1967). The Platform Sutra of the Sixth Patriarch. Columbia University Press. Cleary, T. & Cleary, J.C. (1977). The Blue Cliff Record. Shambhala.

concepts_introduced.note: Huineng — enlightenment does not require gradual cultivation — Chan innovation
Zhaozhou's mu — paradigmatic koan — no conceptual answer possible
Mazu — the ordinary is already enlightened — Chan naturalness

citation: Schimmel, A. (1978). The Triumphal Sun: A Study of the Works of Jalaloddin Rumi. London: Fine Books. Chittick, W.C. (1983). The Sufi Path of Love. SUNY Press.

concepts_introduced.note: annihilation of self in God — Sufi technical term — parallel to but distinct from Buddhist nirvana and Hindu moksha
divine love as cosmic force — Sufi theology of love
God as the object of mystical longing — Sufi poetic theology

citation: Vaudeville, C. (1974). Kabir. Oxford University Press. Hess, L. & Singh, S. (1983). The Bijak of Kabir. San Francisco: North Point Press.

concepts_introduced.note: inner divine sound as direct path — Sant synthesis of Nath yoga and Sufi traditions
devotion to the formless — without images or rituals — Sant tradition synthesis

citation: McLeod, W.H. (1968). Guru Nanak and the Sikh Religion. Oxford University Press. Mandair, A. (2013). Sikhism: A Guide for the Perplexed. Bloomsbury.

concepts_introduced.note: One Reality — opening of Guru Granth Sahib — monotheistic synthesis beyond Hindu-Muslim divide
Wonderful Lord — Sikh name for the divine — not equivalent to either Allah or brahman
remembrance of the divine name — Sikh practice synthesising Hindu japa and Sufi dhikr
selfless service — Sikh ethical foundation

concepts_introduced.note: cessation — third noble truth, the goal of practice
hindrances — five mental factors obstructing meditation
taints/outflows — what liberation eliminates (asava in Pali)
craving — second noble truth, origin of suffering
feeling-tone — pleasant, unpleasant, neutral; basis of reactivity
intention — the volitional factor that constitutes karma
clinging — fourth nidana, sustains rebirth
insight meditation — direct seeing of three characteristics
loving-kindness — first brahmavihara (maitri in Sanskrit)
compassion — second brahmavihara
mind/consciousness — the knowing faculty
ignorance — root cause of suffering (avidya in Sanskrit)
intentional action — Pali form of karma
phenomenon/teaching — Pali form of dharma
ethical conduct — first element of threefold training
delusion — one of three roots of unskillful action
greed — first root of unskillful action
aversion/hatred — second root of unskillful action
